visualMatch.reason: Aura3D reads as a clear neon tunnel flythrough: faceted blue/purple panels converge to a vanishing point with emissive cyan/magenta/yellow segments, visible bloom glow, and depth falloff toward the center — a strong prompt match. The Three.js render is mostly black with scattered floating neon squares and a single thin cyan ring; the dense FogExp2 (0.042) hides the tube wall geometry so no continuous tunnel interior is visible. It reads as disconnected glowing primitives in darkness rather than a tunnel, so it is only symbolic/barely recognizable.

modifiability.reason: Aura3D uses a concise declarative scene graph with named primitives, parameterized ring/side loops, and high-level effects.fog/effects.bloom and camera.dolly calls that are easy to retune. Three.js hand-builds BufferGeometry, panels, lights, composer passes and a camera path sampler — more verbose but well-structured and offering finer low-level control. Both are readily modifiable.

metrics.reason: Both null treated as 0; no API issues in source or notes.
Three.js reached usable render sooner, lower is better.
Smaller gzipped bundle, lower is better.
Aura3D clearly depicts the tunnel; Three.js render hides the tube and reads as scattered primitives.
Both are clearly structured and editable.

reason: Non-tied metrics split evenly: Aura3D wins linesOfUserCode and visualMatch, while Three.js wins timeToFirstUsableRender and bundleSize (2-2). Aura3D is not strictly better on a majority of non-tied metrics, so it does not meet the win threshold; Three.js does not win visual quality, so it does not meet the loss threshold. Per the even-split rule the result is a tie, despite Aura3D's materially stronger visual.
